text: The surgeon carefully dissects the soft tissue from the inter-aortic triangle to preserve the hypogastric nerve. Dissection continues along the ventral side of the abdominal aorta, with gentle traction around the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The hypogastric nerve is preserved at the inter-aortic triangle and near the IMA root. The IMA is dissected and transected 1-2 centimeters away from the root, with soft tissues separated and pushed down. A hemoclip is applied for double clipping on the proximal side, and a 10mm endoclip is used on the distal side of the IMA.

The surgeon uses an energy device to transect the vessel, followed by dissection along the areolar tissue between the sigmoid mesocolon and the gel autophagia. The medial approach is employed, providing ample working space with minimal handling of the sigmoid colon. The dissection reveals the ureter, dumbbells, and long dorsal vessels, continuing at the transition from the mesosigmoid to the mesorectum to ensure safe margins for transection. Dissection proceeds along the left pelvic side, preserving the hypogastric nerve at the sacral promontory level, which lies one to two centimeters medial to the ureter.

The robotic excise system is used to clear soft tissue surrounding the sigmoid colon. The surgeon prepares the GIA stapler for transection, ensuring no surrounding structures are trapped. The proximal and distal ends of the sigmoid colon stump are checked for integrity. The final specimen reveals a 5 cm ulcerating mass with adequate proximal and distal margins, confirming a T3 N2 M0 staging.
